--- SOURCE 1: New Study Finds Grass-Fed Beef Reduces Carbon Footprint ---
URL: https://smallfarms.oregonstate.edu/new-study-finds-grass-fed-beef-reduces-carbon-footprint

SUMMARY:
# New Study Finds Grass-Fed Beef Reduces Carbon Footprint A new modeling study released last month in the report, *What’s Your Beef?*, found evidence to support the environmental benefits of traditional grass-based beef production and finishing. The National Trust, a conservation non-profit in the United Kingdom, determined that grass-based beef production actually had reduced greenhouse gas emissions when the carbon sequestration and storage of grassland pasture was considered. These findings contribute to the list of other established benefits of grass-fed beef production, including the increased biodiversity of pasture ecosystems and improved quality of run-off water from well-managed pastures. For small farmers wanting to integrate environmental and economic sustainable management strategies and consumers demanding local and healthy pasture-raised meat, these findings support the intuitive environmental benefits of grass-based livestock production.

FULL CONTENT:

![Oregon State University](/sites/all/themes/larch/logo.png)

[Apply](https://admissions.oregonstate.edu/apply-choose-application)   [MyCAS](https://agsci.oregonstate.edu/mycas)

# New Study Finds Grass-Fed Beef Reduces Carbon Footprint

**Author:**Elizabeth Murphy, Small Farms Program, Oregon State University

**Publish Date:**Summer 12

## [s12beef.jpg](/file/s12beefjpg)

![What's your beef?](/sites/agscid7/files/s12beef.jpg "What's your beef?")

A new modeling study released last month in the report, *What’s Your Beef?*, found evidence to support the environmental benefits of traditional grass-based beef production and finishing. The National Trust, a conservation non-profit in the United Kingdom, determined that grass-based beef production actually had reduced greenhouse gas emissions when the carbon sequestration and storage of grassland pasture was considered. Furthermore, as grass-fed pastures are often not suitable to intensive crop production, grass farmers make use of marginal land to actually increase food security.

These findings contribute to the list of other established benefits of grass-fed beef production, including the increased biodiversity of pasture ecosystems and improved quality of run-off water from well-managed pastures. The report also touts the scientific evidence of the health benefits of grass-finished beef. Studies have found that meat from grass-finished cattle has up to a third less saturated fats than grain-fed beef and is higher in beneficial fatty acids, such as omega-3. For small farmers wanting to integrate environmental and economic sustainable management strategies and consumers demanding local and healthy pasture-raised meat, these findings support the intuitive environmental benefits of grass-based livestock production.

View the full National Trust report, *What’s Your Beef?*, and it’s summary at <http://www.nationaltrust.org.uk>.

According to the Intergovermental Panel on Climate Change Synthesis Report (2007), agriculture made up over 13% of total human greenhouse gas emissions in 2004. Nonetheless, agriculture has a unique capacity to mitigate climate change through soil carbon sequestration and improved nutrient and manure management. Small farmers, who have the flexibility to implement value-added management and the economic necessity to increase yields through soil quality improvement have the capacity to lead the way on this front.

Small Farms Program  
Oregon State University

--- SOURCE 3: Grass-Fed Livestock: The Benefits of Natural Grazing - Bivatec Ltd ---
URL: https://www.bivatec.com/blog/the-benefits-of-natural-grazing

SUMMARY:
Soil Health and Biodiversity: Grazing animals play a crucial role in maintaining and enhancing soil health. Their grazing stimulates plant

FULL CONTENT:
![Bivatec Ltd](https://www.bivatec.com/assets/images/logo_white_new.PNG)
![Bivatec Ltd](https://www.bivatec.com/assets/images/logo_dark_new.PNG)

# Grass-Fed Livestock: The Benefits of Natural Grazing

![Grass-Fed Livestock: The Benefits of Natural Grazing](https://www.bivatec.com/assets/images/loader.gif)

Revolutionize your farming operations with our apps! No more paperwork headaches. Embrace the future, download now!

[Download on Play Store](https://play.google.com/store/apps/dev?id=6016224474518534712) [Download on App Store](https://apps.apple.com/us/developer/bivatec-ltd/id1695438250)

As a professional veterinarian, I’ve witnessed firsthand the myriad benefits natural grazing provides to livestock, the environment, and human health. The concept of grass-fed goodness extends beyond a mere trend; it represents a holistic approach to animal husbandry that prioritizes livestock's natural behaviors, diets, and welfare.

In this blog, we will explore the profound advantages of natural grazing, shedding light on why it’s a cornerstone of sustainable and ethical farming practices.

### **1. Healthier Livestock: The Foundation of Grass-Fed Goodness**

At the heart of grass-fed farming is the philosophy of letting livestock feed on what they are naturally designed to eat: grass. Unlike grain-fed livestock, which are often confined and fed a diet high in corn and soy, grass-fed animals graze on pastures, consuming a diet that aligns with their natural physiology.

**This fundamental difference in diet has several health benefits for the animals:**

### **2. Environmental Benefits: Sustainable Farming at Its Best**

Natural grazing isn’t just good for the animals; it’s also beneficial for the environment. Grass-fed systems promote sustainable farming practices that enhance ecosystem health and contribute to the fight against climate change.

**a). Soil Health and Biodiversity**:

Grazing animals play a crucial role in maintaining and enhancing soil health. Their grazing stimulates plant growth, and their manure acts as a natural fertilizer, enriching the soil with essential nutrients. This cyclical process supports a diverse range of plant and insect species, fostering a vibrant ecosystem.

**b). Carbon Sequestration:**

Grasslands have a remarkable ability to sequester carbon from the atmosphere, storing it in the soil. When managed correctly, grazing can enhance this process, making grass-fed livestock farming a valuable tool in mitigating greenhouse gas emissions.

**c). Reduced Pollution:**

Unlike intensive feedlot operations that often lead to water and air pollution, grass-fed systems tend to have a much lower environmental footprint. Pasture-based farms typically use fewer chemical inputs such as fertilizers and pesticides, leading to cleaner waterways and healthier ecosystems.

### **3. Economic Benefits for Farmers and Communities**

Grass-fed farming isn’t just beneficial for the animals and the environment; it also presents significant economic opportunities for farmers and rural communities. By adopting grass-fed practices, farmers can often achieve better financial stability and foster local economic growth.

**i). Higher Market Prices:**

Grass-fed meat and dairy products often command premium prices in the marketplace due to their perceived health benefits, ethical considerations, and superior quality. This premium can help farmers achieve higher profit margins, contributing to the economic sustainability of their operations.

**ii). Reduced Input Costs:**

Grass-fed farming can lead to lower costs for feed, as animals graze on natural pastures rather than relying on purchased grain or other feed supplements. This reduction in feed costs can significantly improve the economic viability of farming operations, especially for small-scale farmers.

**iii). Job Creation and Rural Revitalization:**

Grass-fed operations typically require more hands-on management and labor, which can lead to job creation in rural areas. By providing employment opportunities, grass-fed farming can contribute to the revitalization of rural communities, helping to sustain local economies and preserve agricultural traditions.

### **4. Nutritional Advantages for Humans: A Superior Food Source**

From a nutritional standpoint, grass-fed meat and dairy products offer several advantages over their grain-fed counterparts. The benefits of consuming grass-fed products extend from higher nutrient content to better overall health outcomes for consumers.

**a). Higher Levels of Omega-3 Fatty Acids:**

Grass-fed meats are significantly richer in omega-3 fatty acids, which are known for their anti-inflammatory properties and cardiovascular benefits. These fatty acids are crucial for maintaining heart health and reducing the risk of chronic diseases.

**b). Increased Antioxidants and Vitamins:**

Grass-fed products have been shown to contain higher levels of antioxidants, such as Vitamin E and beta-carotene. These compounds play a vital role in protecting the body from oxidative stress and supporting overall health.

**c). Lower in Unhealthy Fats:**

Compared to grain-fed meat, grass-fed meat typically contains less total fat and is lower in unhealthy saturated fats. This makes it a leaner, healthier option for consumers looking to reduce their intake of harmful fats.

### **5. Ethical Considerations: Promoting Animal Welfare**

A grass-fed system aligns closely with ethical farming practices that prioritize animal welfare. By allowing animals to graze and express their natural behaviors, farmers create an environment that respects the intrinsic value of the animals they raise.

***Natural Living Conditions:*** Grass-fed livestock are typically raised in open pastures, providing them with ample space to move freely, graze, and interact socially. This is in stark contrast to the confined conditions of feedlot systems, where animals often have limited space and restricted movement.

***Reduced Stress and Enhanced Well-being:*** Animals raised on pasture are generally subject to less stress than those in intensive farming systems. Lower stress levels contribute to better overall health and a reduced risk of disease, leading to a more humane and sustainable farming practice.

***Transparency and Consumer Trust:*** Farmers who adopt grass-fed systems are often more transparent about their practices, fostering trust between them and their consumers. This transparency allows consumers to make informed choices about their products, supporting ethical and sustainable farming practices.

### **6. Challenges and Myths of Grass-Fed Farming**

While grass-fed farming offers numerous benefits, it’s not without its challenges and misconceptions. Understanding these challenges is crucial for farmers, consumers, and policymakers to make informed decisions and to promote realistic expectations about grass-fed practices.

**a). Land and Resource Management:**

One of the primary challenges in grass-fed farming is ensuring sustainable land use. Overgrazing can lead to soil degradation and reduced pasture productivity if not managed correctly. Farmers must implement rotational grazing and other regenerative practices to maintain pasture health.

**b). Seasonal Variability and Climate Impact:**

Grass-fed systems are heavily dependent on climatic conditions, which can affect pasture growth and quality. In regions with harsh winters or prolonged dry spells, maintaining a consistent grass-fed diet can be challenging, necessitating the use of stored forage or supplemental feeding.

**c). Consumer Misconceptions and Marketing Myths:**

There are several myths surrounding grass-fed products, such as the belief that all grass-fed meat is organic or that it is always better in taste and texture.

While grass-fed meat has many benefits, it can vary significantly depending on factors like breed, age, and specific farm practices. Educating consumers about these nuances is essential to prevent unrealistic expectations and to promote a more informed market.

**d). Higher Production Costs:**

While input costs like feed may be lower, grass-fed farming can incur higher costs in other areas, such as land management, labor, and longer production times. These factors can make it financially challenging for some farmers to transition to or maintain grass-fed operations, especially without adequate market access or support.

### **7. The Future of Grass-Fed Farming: Challenges and Opportunities**

While the benefits of grass-fed farming are clear, some challenges must be addressed to ensure its continued success and growth. These include managing land resources sustainably, addressing economic viability for farmers, and meeting the increasing demand for grass-fed products.

**i). Balancing Productivity with Sustainability:**

One of the primary challenges is balancing the need for productivity with the principles of sustainability. Farmers must adopt regenerative grazing practices that prevent overgrazing and ensure the long-term health of pastures.

**ii). Economic Considerations:**

Grass-fed farming can be more labor-intensive and may require a larger land base compared to conventional systems. Farmers must navigate these economic challenges to make grass-fed farming financially viable while maintaining its core principles.

**iii). Education and Advocacy:**

Educating consumers and policymakers about the benefits of grass-fed systems is crucial. Increased awareness can drive demand for grass-fed products and support policy changes that encourage sustainable farming practices.

### **Conclusion**

Grass-fed goodness embodies a comprehensive approach to livestock farming that benefits animals, humans, and the environment. By embracing natural grazing, we not only enhance the well-being of livestock but also contribute to a more sustainable and ethical food system.

As we look to the future, we must continue to support and advocate for grass-fed farming, ensuring that its benefits can be realized for generations to come.

By championing grass-fed systems, we are not only choosing better health and nutrition but also for a healthier planet and a more humane approach to animal agriculture. The path to sustainable farming lies in recognizing the intrinsic value of animals and the ecosystems they inhabit—a vision that is embodied in the very essence of grass-fed goodness.

![American Grassfed Association](https://www.americangrassfed.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/aga-dark-logo.png)

# Why Grassfed Matters: The Environmental Benefits Of Pasture-Based Farming

[blog](https://www.americangrassfed.org/category/blog/)

![](https://i0.wp.com/www.americangrassfed.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/12/Untitled-design-76-3.png?resize=1080%2C540&ssl=1)

![](https://i0.wp.com/www.americangrassfed.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/12/Untitled-design-76-3.png?resize=1080%2C540&ssl=1)

## 

Grassfed and pasture-based farming isn’t just a trend in sustainable agriculture; it’s an essential approach that positively impacts our environment. The [American Grassfed Association (AGA)](https://www.americangrassfed.org/) supports farmers who are committed to grassfed and pasture-based practices, which foster biodiversity, reduce greenhouse gasses, and promote healthier ecosystems. This article explores why grassfed matters and how pasture-based farming, including practices for beef, dairy hogs, poultry, and other AGA-certified species, is paving the way for a more sustainable and resilient food system.

## **1. Soil Health And Regeneration**

One of the primary environmental benefits of grassfed, pasture-based farming is its effect on soil health. Conventional farming often relies on confined animal feeding operations (CAFOs) and grain-based diets, grassfed practices allow animals to graze freely on pastures, which enriches the soil.

**How it Works**

When animals graze on pasture, their hooves naturally aerate the soil, allowing water and nutrients to penetrate deeper. The natural grazing process promotes the growth of diverse plant species, which, in turn, improves soil fertility and structure. Additionally, organic matter accumulates in the soil, supporting carbon sequestration—a critical process in mitigating climate change by capturing carbon dioxide and storing it in the ground.

This cycle of grazing and regrowth not only improves soil resilience but also reduces erosion and water runoff, especially when animals are rotationally grazed across different areas of the pasture.

### **Cited Studies**

A study on White Oak Pastures found that regenerative grazing results in [-3.5 kg CO2-eq emissions per kilogram of fresh meat compared to U.S](https://blog.whiteoakpastures.com/hubfs/WOP-LCA-Quantis-2019.pdf). conventional farming practices. This highlights a substantial reduction in greenhouse gas emissions associated with regenerative grazing.

![](https://i0.wp.com/www.americangrassfed.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/12/Untitled-design-77-2.png?resize=1080%2C540&ssl=1)

![](https://i0.wp.com/www.americangrassfed.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/12/Untitled-design-77-2.png?resize=1080%2C540&ssl=1)

## **2. Biodiversity And Wildlife Habitat**

Grassfed, pasture-based farming systems help create and sustain biodiversity. By fostering a pasture environment where animals graze freely, farmers can protect and promote a variety of plants, insects, and other species. This rich biodiversity is especially evident when compared to monoculture crops used in conventional feed systems, which often deplete natural habitats and biodiversity.

For example, birds, bees, and other pollinators benefit immensely from the flowering plants in pasture environments. This biodiversity also supports predator-prey relationships and soil microbes, all of which contribute to a more resilient ecosystem.

### **Species Inclusion**

[AGA certification](https://www.americangrassfed.org/become-a-certified-producer/) covers multiple species, including hogs and poultry, which benefit from pasture-based living even with necessary supplemental feeding. This diverse system makes pasture-based farming inclusive of all animals, contributing to a well-rounded ecosystem that’s rich in different species and nutrients.

### **Environmental Impact of Grassfed Beef and Other Species**

Grassfed beef farming can not only reduce methane emissions by promoting carbon sequestration but also eliminates the need for chemical fertilizers, pesticides, and antibiotics. Hogs and poultry, although allowed supplemental feeding, benefit from the open pasture environment. 

Research shows that [pasture-based systems for beef and other animals reduce greenhouse gas emissions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1751731120001822#:~:text=Recovery%20and%20intensification%20of%20pasture%2Dbased%20beef%20cattle%20production%20systems%20simultaneously%20improve%20carbon%20sequestration%20and%20mitigate%20greenhouse%20gases%20emissions), proving the impact of sustainable grazing.

## **3. Water Conservation And Quality**

Water usage and conservation are critical issues in agriculture, and grassfed farming supports these efforts in significant ways. Pasture-based systems generally require less water than conventional feedlots. Instead of growing water-intensive grains to feed animals, pasture-based farms rely on natural grasslands that are more suited to local water cycles and rainfall.

### **Water Quality Benefits**

The natural process of grazing prevents large-scale runoff that can pollute water bodies with contaminants. Animal waste in pasture-based systems is distributed evenly across the land, allowing it to decompose naturally and enrich the soil.

## **4. Supporting Rural Economies And Sustainable Farming**

Pasture-based farming supports family farms and rural economies by enabling small-scale farmers to raise animals in a sustainable way. This approach also encourages farmers to use fewer external inputs, such as commercial feed and chemical fertilizers, which often come at a high financial and environmental cost. By advocating for grassfed and pasture-based certifications across various species, the [American Grassfed Association](https://www.americangrassfed.org/) helps create market demand for sustainable products, boosting rural economies and providing consumers with environmentally responsible options.

## **Additional Benefits for Hogs and Poultry**

[AGA’s certification](https://www.americangrassfed.org/become-a-certified-producer/) includes hogs and poultry, which, although primarily pasture-based, are allowed dietary supplementation to ensure they thrive. This approach balances animal welfare with sustainability, creating a model for resilient farming that can support different types of livestock.

## 

![](https://i0.wp.com/www.americangrassfed.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/12/Untitled-design-78-2.png?resize=1080%2C540&ssl=1)

## **Join The Grassfed Movement**

Grassfed and pasture-based farming offers substantial environmental benefits—from promoting soil health and biodiversity to reducing greenhouse gas emissions and conserving water. By supporting pasture-based methods for all certified species, including hogs and poultry, the [American Grassfed Association](https://www.americangrassfed.org/) is championing a sustainable model of agriculture that benefits ecosystems, rural economies, and the planet.

### **Get Involved with AGA**

Learn more about the [American Grassfed Association’s certifications](https://www.americangrassfed.org/become-a-certified-producer/), partnerships, and advocacy efforts by visiting [americangrassfed.org](https://www.americangrassfed.org/). Together, we can make a difference by supporting sustainable practices that matter—for our health, communities, and environment. [Join us in promoting grassfed and pasture-based farming](https://www.americangrassfed.org/become-member-forms/) for a greener, healthier future.

--- SOURCE 5: How Grassfed Farming Supports Biodiversity and Soil Health ---
URL: https://www.americangrassfed.org/how-grassfed-farming-supports-biodiversity-and-soil-health/

SUMMARY:
This practice prevents overgrazing, allows plant recovery, and creates varied habitats that attract different wildlife species. Studies published in

FULL CONTENT:
"\*" indicates required fields

![American Grassfed Association](https://www.americangrassfed.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/aga-dark-logo.png)

# How Grassfed Farming Supports Biodiversity and Soil Health

[blog](https://www.americangrassfed.org/category/blog/)

![](https://i1.wp.com/www.americangrassfed.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Importance-of-Estate-Planning-for-Families-58.png?resize=1080%2C569&ssl=1)

![](https://i1.wp.com/www.americangrassfed.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Importance-of-Estate-Planning-for-Families-58.png?resize=1080%2C569&ssl=1)

Grassfed farming practices have far-reaching benefits beyond providing healthier meat, milk, and eggs. These sustainable agricultural methods play a critical role in supporting biodiversity and improving soil health. 

By prioritizing pasture-based systems for various species—including cattle, sheep, goats, hogs, and poultry—grassfed farming enhances ecosystem resilience and contributes to the long-term health of our planet.

## **Biodiversity: A Keystone of Healthy Ecosystems**

Grassfed farming fosters biodiversity by creating an environment where multiple plant and animal species can thrive. Traditional monoculture farming often eliminates native species, reducing the complexity of ecosystems. In contrast, grass systems encourage the growth of diverse plant species in pastures, which in turn support a variety of insects, birds, and small mammals.

Research by the [Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO)](https://openknowledge.fao.org/server/api/core/bitstreams/fd57ac5a-5c31-4379-968d-5866a3411f87/content) highlights that maintaining pasture-based systems allows for the preservation of habitats critical to pollinators, such as bees and butterflies. Pollinators play an essential role in food production, and their presence underscores the interconnectedness of grassfed farming and ecological stability.

Additionally, pasture-based grazing systems often incorporate rotational grazing, where livestock are moved between different pasture sections. This practice prevents overgrazing, allows plant recovery, and creates varied habitats that attract different wildlife species. Studies published in [*ScienceDirect*](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/rotational-grazing) confirm that rotational grazing enhances biodiversity compared to continuous grazing or confinement-based livestock systems.

## 

![](https://i0.wp.com/www.americangrassfed.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Importance-of-Estate-Planning-for-Families-60.png?resize=1080%2C569&ssl=1)

## **Soil Health: A Foundation for Sustainability**

Healthy soil is the cornerstone of agricultural productivity and environmental resilience. Grassfed farming practices actively improve soil health through natural processes that enrich the land. Livestock grazing stimulates root growth in pasture plants, which increases organic matter and improves soil structure.

Grass systems also enhance soil’s water retention capacity. As diverse grasses and legumes flourish, their root systems create channels that help water infiltrate and reduce runoff. This is especially beneficial in preventing soil erosion, a significant problem in conventional farming systems.

Furthermore, livestock manure in pasture-based systems contributes to natural fertilization. Unlike the concentrated waste of feedlots, manure dispersed across pastures introduces essential nutrients back into the soil without overwhelming it. Research from the [Rodale Institute indicates](https://rodaleinstitute.org/wp-content/uploads/rodale-white-paper.pdf) that pasture-based systems have the potential to sequester carbon, helping mitigate climate change by reducing greenhouse gas levels in the atmosphere.

Notably, the [life cycle assessment (LCA) conducted at White Oak Pastures](https://blog.whiteoakpastures.com/blog/carbon-negative-grassfed-beef) demonstrates the significant potential of grassfed farming to sequester carbon. White Oak Pastures’ regenerative practices, including rotational grazing and diverse pasture management, have been shown to result in carbon-negative beef production. According to the LCA, these practices sequester more carbon in the soil than the entire beef production process emits, highlighting how grassfed farming can actively combat climate change by reducing greenhouse gas levels in the atmosphere.

This research underscores that grass systems not only sustain but enhance soil health, contributing to a more resilient and sustainable agricultural future.

## **Pasture-Based Systems for All Species**

The benefits of grassfed farming extend to all species certified by the [American Grassfed Association (AGA)](https://www.americangrassfed.org/). While beef cattle are commonly associated with grass systems, it’s essential to recognize the inclusion of sheep, goats, hogs, and poultry.

Hogs and poultry in pasture-based systems primarily rely on grazing but can be supplemented as necessary to meet their nutritional needs. This distinction ensures animal welfare while promoting environmental benefits. Pasture-based systems for these species still support biodiversity and soil health by encouraging natural behaviors such as rooting and foraging, which aerate the soil and contribute to plant diversity.

## **Scientific Backing for Grassfed Practices**

Multiple studies provide evidence supporting the positive environmental impacts of grassfed farming. For example, a study in [*Science of The Total Environment*](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969721009992) demonstrated that diversified pasture systems can reduce greenhouse gas emissions compared to grain-fed livestock operations. Similarly, the [Savory Institute](https://savory.global/what-is-holistic-management/) has documented how holistic grazing management can reverse desertification, further emphasizing the role of pasture-based systems in ecosystem restoration

![](https://i1.wp.com/www.americangrassfed.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Importance-of-Estate-Planning-for-Families-59.png?resize=1080%2C569&ssl=1).

![](https://i1.wp.com/www.americangrassfed.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Importance-of-Estate-Planning-for-Families-59.png?resize=1080%2C569&ssl=1)

## **Supporting a Sustainable Future**

Grassfed farming is more than a sustainable agricultural practice—it is a path to healthier ecosystems, enriched soil, and thriving biodiversity. By choosing products certified by the [American Grassfed Association](https://www.americangrassfed.org/), consumers directly support farmers and ranchers dedicated to these environmentally responsible methods.

Ready to explore how grassfed farming can transform your understanding of sustainable agriculture? Visit [the American Grassfed Association](https://www.americangrassfed.org/) to learn more about certification, advocacy, and how you can contribute to a sustainable future.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Grass-fed farming fosters diverse ecosystems by preserving natural habitats, supporting various plant and animal species that thrive in open pastures compared to monoculture systems.
Grasslands managed through grazing can sequester carbon effectively, capturing atmospheric carbon dioxide and reducing greenhouse gas emissions while enhancing soil health.
Sustainable grazing enhances ecosystem services, such as improved water quality, reduced erosion, and increased resilience, promoting healthier landscapes through natural nutrient cycling.
